scenarios.task: A discount function applies a discount when order total is between $50 and $500 inclusive. Our test suite has one test case using a $200 order, confirms the discount applies, and code coverage reports 100% line coverage on the function. Are we good to ship?
criteria: States that 100% line coverage does not mean the logic was correctly tested — coverage measures what ran, not whether the right assertions/boundaries were checked
Identifies that a mid-range-only test can pass while a boundary bug (e.g. > instead of >=) goes completely undetected
Recommends adding the boundary cases to the regression suite specifically, not just spot-checking once
Does not treat 100% coverage as sufficient evidence to ship without boundary testing

scenarios.task: Our test suite has 40 unit tests, 30 integration tests, and 200 end-to-end browser tests. CI takes 45 minutes and about 15% of E2E tests fail intermittently on any given run — the team just reruns the pipeline until it goes green. Review this setup.
criteria: Flags the suite shape as an inverted 'ice cream cone' — too many E2E tests relative to unit tests — versus the test pyramid's broad-unit-base model
Explains that this shape produces slow, hard-to-diagnose failures since an E2E failure could be caused by almost anything
Flags rerunning until green as the wrong response to flaky tests, since it erodes trust in the suite's signal and can hide a real intermittent bug
Recommends triaging each flaky test explicitly — root-cause and fix, or quarantine with a tracked ticket — not simply rerunning
Recommends rebalancing the suite toward more unit/integration tests and fewer E2E tests going forward

scenarios.task: We found two bugs during testing: (1) the settings page's font size is slightly inconsistent on one browser, and (2) the checkout page occasionally double-charges a customer's card under high load. QA wants to classify both the same way since 'a bug's a bug.' How would you classify and prioritize these?
criteria: Classifies severity (technical impact) and priority (business impact) as two separate dimensions, not a single blended rating
Rates the double-charge bug as high severity (data/financial impact) and high priority (direct revenue/trust exposure)
Rates the font inconsistency as low severity (cosmetic, no data/crash impact) with correspondingly low priority
Explicitly pushes back on treating both bugs identically just because both are 'a bug'
Recommends prioritizing test/fix effort toward the double-charge issue given the risk (probability x impact) differential, not spreading equal effort across both
